content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To      : Nancy Conzett                                Date  : December 29, 1977

                                                       77-12-089

From  : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
        Dwight Dickerson

Subject: Phone Advice


Our office received a letter from Menlo Park Council Member
Jennifer Bigelow dated December 8, 1977. In that letter she
asked us to consider the following: In the near future, a
matter pertaining to the development of a parcel of land
adjacent to a piece of property upon which she resides will
be considered by the Council. The question she raises is
whether she is disqualified from participating in the matter
pertaining to the development.

On December 22, 1977, I advised her by phone that Government
Code §§87100 and 87103(b) stated that she had to disqualify
herself from participating in a governmental decision where
it is foreseeable that the decision could have a material
effect on her property interest. I pointed out to her that
the Commission offered guidelines in Regulation 18702(b)(2)
for determining when a material financial effect would be
present.

I made it clear to Council Member Bigelow that the guidelines
offered by the Commission were not conclusive. The Commission
stated in 18702(a) that if the official knows or has reason to
know that their financial interest could impair their ability
to be free from bias, materiality would be present, and the
official would probably have to be disqualified if the other
elements were present.


DD:ma

.ER J. STEPHENS
.MAYOR

JENNIFER BIGELOW
MAYOR PRO TEM

JAMES L. BLOCH
COUNCILMEMBER

IRA E. BONDE
COUNCILMEMBER

JAMES W. CALLOWAY
COUNCILMEMBER

CITY OF
MENLO
PARK

CIVIC CENTER / MENLO PARK, CALIFORNIA 94025 / TELEPHONE (415) 325-3211

December 8, 1977

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, Calif. 95804

Gentlemen:

I am a member of the Menlo Park City Council.

In the near future, a matter will be brought before
the City Council pertaining to the development of a
parcel of land adjacent to a piece of property where
I reside.

I have no financial or real interests in the parcel
subject to development.

As a member of the Council, should I disqualify my-
self from participating in making a decision affect-
ing this property?

I am requesting your ruling in order to avoid the
possibility of a "conflict of interest" controversy
arising.

I look forward to hearing from you at your earliest
convenience.

Sincerely,

Jennifer Bigelow
Council Member

JB/lb

sections.question: Whether a Menlo Park Council Member is disqualified from participating in a matter pertaining to the development of a parcel of land adjacent to a piece of property upon which she resides.
sections.conclusion: Government Code §§87100 and 87103(b) stated that she had to disqualify herself from participating in a governmental decision where it is foreseeable that the decision could have a material effect on her property interest. The Commission's Regulation 18702(b)(2) provides guidelines for determining when a material financial effect would be present, though these guidelines are not conclusive. Under Commission Regulation 18702(a), if the official knows or has reason to know that their financial interest could impair their ability to be free from bias, materiality would be present, and the official would probably have to be disqualified if the other elements were present.
sections.question_synthetic: A Menlo Park Council Member asked whether she must disqualify herself from voting on a land development matter involving a parcel adjacent to her own property.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised the Council Member that she must disqualify herself under Government Code §§87100 and 87103(b) because the land development decision could foreseeably have a material effect on her adjacent property. The determination of materiality depends on whether the official knows or has reason to know that their financial interest could impair their objectivity, guided by Regulation 18702.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a summary of phone advice given on December 22, 1977, documented in a letter dated December 29, 1977. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' headers present but clear question and conclusion sections exist in narrative form. City letterhead present suggesting this may be a copy sent to city officials.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a Menlo Park Council Member is disqualified from participating in a matter pertaining to the development of a parcel of land adjacent to a piece of property upon which she resides.

CONCLUSION: Government Code §§87100 and 87103(b) stated that she had to disqualify herself from participating in a governmental decision where it is foreseeable that the decision could have a material effect on her property interest. The Commission's Regulation 18702(b)(2) provides guidelines for determining when a material financial effect would be present, though these guidelines are not conclusive. Under Commission Regulation 18702(a), if the official knows or has reason to know that their financial interest could impair their ability to be free from bias, materiality would be present, and the official would probably have to be disqualified if the other elements were present.

The question she raises is whether she is disqualified from participating in the matter pertaining to the development. On December 22, 1977, I advised her by phone that Government Code §§87100 and 87103(b) stated that she had to disqualify herself from participating in a governmental decision where it is foreseeable that the decision could have a material effect on her property interest. embedding.summary: The FPPC provided phone advice to Menlo Park Council Member Jennifer Bigelow regarding her disqualification requirements for a vote on a land development matter affecting property adjacent to her residence. The FPPC advised that she must disqualify herself under Government Code §§87100 and 87103(b) because the decision could foreseeably have a material effect on her property interest.
